cards.name: Tempo Bodyweight Squat

scoring.scoringNote: Strength stays primary. Explosiveness is secondary and delivery-dependent; never author as plyometrics, burpees, jumps, or conditioning-first work.

cards.cardSummary: Slow-tempo squat that turns air-squat leverage into a real strength stimulus through time under tension, bracing, depth ownership, and repeatable knee/hip alignment. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent, not the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with enough rest that strength-quality reps do not turn into a bodyweight burnout circuit.
cards.description: Tempo Bodyweight Squat is a tempo/eccentric bodyweight strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: squat / knee / dominant / strength. Score Strength high. Add Explosiveness only as a small secondary score when the coach prescribes crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat this as a plyometric, burpee, jump, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly. Strong and sharp beats rushed and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: Tempo Bodyweight Squat uses bodyweight leverage as the resistance. The athlete must organize joints, trunk tension, and range while producing force against their own body mass, making the drill useful for relative strength without external loading.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when delivered with high intent, but it should not replace true Output plyometrics or speed work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a bodyweight strength drill into a fast fatigue circuit because no external load is present. Keep tempo, leverage, range, rest, and rep quality aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by leverage, range of motion, tempo, pause length, assistance, support height, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality remain stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tempo Bodyweight Squat is a tempo/eccentric bodyweight strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
setup: Clear a non-slip training space and choose a variation the athlete can control without pain.
Set the starting body line before the first rep: brace, align the target joints, and keep the face/neck relaxed.
Use a stance and range of motion that the athlete can repeat with knee, hip, ankle, and trunk control.
execution_steps: Start each repetition from a still, organized position.
Lower slowly through the prescribed range; do not drop, bounce, or rush the eccentric.
Drive through the concentric portion with strong intent while keeping the same body line and joint path.
Reset at the top or start position before the next rep so every rep looks repeatable.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the hard part when needed.
Brace without panic breathing.
Exhale through the sticking point or after the rep without collapsing.
common_faults: Chasing fatigue or rep count instead of strength-quality reps.
Knees collapse inward or heels lift without control.
Bouncing out of the bottom instead of producing force.
Torso position changes to survive the rep.
quality_gate: No pain or joint collapse.
Start and finish positions match the intended variation.
Final rep looks close to the first rep.
Coach would be comfortable progressing leverage, range, or volume next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Athlete uses momentum, swing, bounce, or impact to survive the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the simplest leverage and shortest range that can be owned. Keep sets short, coach one cue at a time, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add range, pause, or a slightly harder leverage only after the athlete can repeat the setup and finish with control. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress leverage, range, tempo, and total volume gradually. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative range, assistance, higher support, or fewer reps. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding difficulty.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress through harder leverage, longer range, pauses, slow eccentrics, unilateral emphasis, or reduced assistance while preserving adequate rest and strength intent.
scaling.older_adult: Use higher supports, reduced range, slower controlled tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain, breath holding, and positions that irritate joints.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance when appropriate and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, leaking, or pain.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, joint history, and current capacity rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: True Output work if included earlier in the session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only if movement quality remains safe
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the target pattern.
Athlete cannot control bodyweight safely in the chosen variation.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex bodyweight strength demo recommended
Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Capacity / strength sequencing rules
ACSM-style resistance training technique and progression guidance
mediaInternalNotes: Film Tempo Bodyweight Squat from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics or conditioning.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the easier regression without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
Training area and support equipment are stable and clear.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the selected pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe setup for the required support, bar, wall, rings, or floor space.

cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when delivered with high intent, but it should not replace true Output plyometrics or speed work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a bodyweight strength drill into a fast fatigue circuit because no external load is present. Keep tempo, leverage, range, rest, and rep quality aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by leverage, range of motion, tempo, pause length, assistance, support height, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality remain stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Bodyweight Reverse Lunge is a lower-body bodyweight strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
setup: Clear a non-slip training space and choose a variation the athlete can control without pain.
Set the starting body line before the first rep: brace, align the target joints, and keep the face/neck relaxed.
Use a stance and range of motion that the athlete can repeat with knee, hip, ankle, and trunk control.
execution_steps: Start each repetition from a still, organized position.
Move through the strength range with control, using only as much speed as the coaching intent allows.
Drive through the concentric portion with strong intent while keeping the same body line and joint path.
Reset at the top or start position before the next rep so every rep looks repeatable.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the hard part when needed.
Brace without panic breathing.
Exhale through the sticking point or after the rep without collapsing.
common_faults: Chasing fatigue or rep count instead of strength-quality reps.
Knees collapse inward or heels lift without control.
Bouncing out of the bottom instead of producing force.
Torso position changes to survive the rep.
quality_gate: No pain or joint collapse.
Start and finish positions match the intended variation.
Final rep looks close to the first rep.
Coach would be comfortable progressing leverage, range, or volume next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Athlete uses momentum, swing, bounce, or impact to survive the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the simplest leverage and shortest range that can be owned. Keep sets short, coach one cue at a time, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add range, pause, or a slightly harder leverage only after the athlete can repeat the setup and finish with control. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress leverage, range, tempo, and total volume gradually. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative range, assistance, higher support, or fewer reps. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding difficulty.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress through harder leverage, longer range, pauses, slow eccentrics, unilateral emphasis, or reduced assistance while preserving adequate rest and strength intent.
scaling.older_adult: Use higher supports, reduced range, slower controlled tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain, breath holding, and positions that irritate joints.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance when appropriate and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, leaking, or pain.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, joint history, and current capacity rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: True Output work if included earlier in the session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only if movement quality remains safe
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the target pattern.
Athlete cannot control bodyweight safely in the chosen variation.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.

cards.name: Tempo Push-Up

scoring.scoringNote: Strength stays primary. Explosiveness is secondary and delivery-dependent; never author as plyometrics, burpees, jumps, or conditioning-first work.

cards.cardSummary: Slow eccentric and controlled press turn a standard push-up into a stronger upper-body and trunk strength stimulus. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent, not the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with enough rest that strength-quality reps do not turn into a bodyweight burnout circuit.
cards.description: Tempo Push-Up is a tempo/eccentric bodyweight strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: upper / body / push / strength. Score Strength high. Add Explosiveness only as a small secondary score when the coach prescribes crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat this as a plyometric, burpee, jump, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly. Strong and sharp beats rushed and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: Tempo Push-Up uses bodyweight leverage as the resistance. The athlete must organize joints, trunk tension, and range while producing force against their own body mass, making the drill useful for relative strength without external loading.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when delivered with high intent, but it should not replace true Output plyometrics or speed work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a bodyweight strength drill into a fast fatigue circuit because no external load is present. Keep tempo, leverage, range, rest, and rep quality aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by leverage, range of motion, tempo, pause length, assistance, support height, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality remain stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tempo Push-Up is a tempo/eccentric bodyweight strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
setup: Use a mat if the position places pressure on knees, wrists, back, or elbows.
Set the starting body line before the first rep: brace, align the target joints, and keep the face/neck relaxed.
Place hands so wrists, elbows, shoulders, and trunk can stay organized through the full range.
execution_steps: Start each repetition from a still, organized position.
Lower slowly through the prescribed range; do not drop, bounce, or rush the eccentric.
Drive through the concentric portion with strong intent while keeping the same body line and joint path.
Reset at the top or start position before the next rep so every rep looks repeatable.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the hard part when needed.
Brace without panic breathing.
Exhale through the sticking point or after the rep without collapsing.
common_faults: Chasing fatigue or rep count instead of strength-quality reps.
Sagging or piking the hips to make the press easier.
Shoulders dump forward or elbows flare painfully.
Cutting range without a planned reason.
quality_gate: No pain or joint collapse.
Start and finish positions match the intended variation.
Final rep looks close to the first rep.
Coach would be comfortable progressing leverage, range, or volume next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Athlete uses momentum, swing, bounce, or impact to survive the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the simplest leverage and shortest range that can be owned. Keep sets short, coach one cue at a time, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add range, pause, or a slightly harder leverage only after the athlete can repeat the setup and finish with control. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress leverage, range, tempo, and total volume gradually. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative range, assistance, higher support, or fewer reps. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding difficulty.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress through harder leverage, longer range, pauses, slow eccentrics, unilateral emphasis, or reduced assistance while preserving adequate rest and strength intent.
scaling.older_adult: Use higher supports, reduced range, slower controlled tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain, breath holding, and positions that irritate joints.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance when appropriate and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, leaking, or pain.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, joint history, and current capacity rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: True Output work if included earlier in the session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only if movement quality remains safe
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the target pattern.
Athlete cannot control bodyweight safely in the chosen variation.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.

cards.whyItWorks: Pseudo-Planche Push-Up uses bodyweight leverage as the resistance. The athlete must organize joints, trunk tension, and range while producing force against their own body mass, making the drill useful for relative strength without external loading.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Pseudo-Planche Push-Up is a bodyweight pressing strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
setup: Clear a non-slip training space and choose a variation the athlete can control without pain.
Set the starting body line before the first rep: brace, align the target joints, and keep the face/neck relaxed.
Place hands so wrists, elbows, shoulders, and trunk can stay organized through the full range.
execution_steps: Start each repetition from a still, organized position.
Move through the strength range with control, using only as much speed as the coaching intent allows.
Drive through the concentric portion with strong intent while keeping the same body line and joint path.
Reset at the top or start position before the next rep so every rep looks repeatable.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the hard part when needed.
Brace without panic breathing.
Exhale through the sticking point or after the rep without collapsing.
common_faults: Chasing fatigue or rep count instead of strength-quality reps.
Sagging or piking the hips to make the press easier.
Shoulders dump forward or elbows flare painfully.
Cutting range without a planned reason.
quality_gate: No pain or joint collapse.
Start and finish positions match the intended variation.
Final rep looks close to the first rep.
Coach would be comfortable progressing leverage, range, or volume next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Athlete uses momentum, swing, bounce, or impact to survive the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the simplest leverage and shortest range that can be owned. Keep sets short, coach one cue at a time, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add range, pause, or a slightly harder leverage only after the athlete can repeat the setup and finish with control. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress leverage, range, tempo, and total volume gradually. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative range, assistance, higher support, or fewer reps. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding difficulty.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress through harder leverage, longer range, pauses, slow eccentrics, unilateral emphasis, or reduced assistance while preserving adequate rest and strength intent.
scaling.older_adult: Use higher supports, reduced range, slower controlled tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain, breath holding, and positions that irritate joints.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance when appropriate and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, leaking, or pain.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, joint history, and current capacity rather than gender alone.

pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only if movement quality remains safe

cards.name: Archer Pull-Up

scoring.scoringNote: Strength stays primary. Explosiveness is secondary and delivery-dependent; never author as plyometrics, burpees, jumps, or conditioning-first work.

cards.cardSummary: Asymmetrical pull-up that overloads one side and bridges toward one-arm pulling strength without requiring kipping or explosive swing. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent, not the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with enough rest that strength-quality reps do not turn into a bodyweight burnout circuit.
cards.description: Archer Pull-Up is a bodyweight pulling strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: pull / hang / grip / strength. Score Strength high. Add Explosiveness only as a small secondary score when the coach prescribes crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat this as a plyometric, burpee, jump, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly. Strong and sharp beats rushed and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: Archer Pull-Up uses bodyweight leverage as the resistance. The athlete must organize joints, trunk tension, and range while producing force against their own body mass, making the drill useful for relative strength without external loading.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when delivered with high intent, but it should not replace true Output plyometrics or speed work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a bodyweight strength drill into a fast fatigue circuit because no external load is present. Keep tempo, leverage, range, rest, and rep quality aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by leverage, range of motion, tempo, pause length, assistance, support height, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality remain stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Archer Pull-Up is a bodyweight pulling strength exercise. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
setup: Check that the bar, rings, straps, or support are secure and appropriate for the athlete's size and strength.
Set the starting body line before the first rep: brace, align the target joints, and keep the face/neck relaxed.
Set the grip, shoulder position, and hollow or lightly braced body line before pulling or holding.
execution_steps: Start each repetition from a still, organized position.
Move through the strength range with control, using only as much speed as the coaching intent allows.
Drive through the concentric portion with strong intent while keeping the same body line and joint path.
Reset at the top or start position before the next rep so every rep looks repeatable.
coach_cues: Pull the chest toward the target.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the hard part when needed.
Brace without panic breathing.
Exhale through the sticking point or after the rep without collapsing.
common_faults: Chasing fatigue or rep count instead of strength-quality reps.
Kipping, kicking, or swinging to finish the rep.
Losing active shoulder position at the bottom.
Chin reaches while ribs flare or neck strains.
quality_gate: No pain or joint collapse.
Start and finish positions match the intended variation.
Final rep looks close to the first rep.
Coach would be comfortable progressing leverage, range, or volume next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Athlete uses momentum, swing, bounce, or impact to survive the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the simplest leverage and shortest range that can be owned. Keep sets short, coach one cue at a time, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add range, pause, or a slightly harder leverage only after the athlete can repeat the setup and finish with control. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress leverage, range, tempo, and total volume gradually. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative range, assistance, higher support, or fewer reps. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding difficulty.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress through harder leverage, longer range, pauses, slow eccentrics, unilateral emphasis, or reduced assistance while preserving adequate rest and strength intent.
scaling.older_adult: Use higher supports, reduced range, slower controlled tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain, breath holding, and positions that irritate joints.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance when appropriate and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, leaking, or pain.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, joint history, and current capacity rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: True Output work if included earlier in the session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only if movement quality remains safe
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the target pattern.
Athlete cannot control bodyweight safely in the chosen variation.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.

cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: tissue / capacity / isometric / eccentric / accessory. Score Strength high. Add Explosiveness only as a small secondary score when the coach prescribes crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat this as a plyometric, burpee, jump, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly. Strong and sharp beats rushed and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: L-Sit uses bodyweight leverage as the resistance. The athlete must organize joints, trunk tension, and range while producing force against their own body mass, making the drill useful for relative strength without external loading.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when delivered with high intent, but it should not replace true Output plyometrics or speed work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a bodyweight strength drill into a fast fatigue circuit because no external load is present. Keep tempo, leverage, range, rest, and rep quality aligned with strength first. Do not tag it as explosiveness just because the athlete is working hard; the card is mainly isometric/eccentric strength and tissue capacity.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by leverage, range of motion, tempo, pause length, assistance, support height, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality remain stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: L-Sit is a isometric bodyweight strength hold. Use leverage, range, tempo, pause, and rest to create force production instead of conditioning fatigue.
setup: Check that the bar, rings, straps, or support are secure and appropriate for the athlete's size and strength.
Set the starting body line before the first rep: brace, align the target joints, and keep the face/neck relaxed.
Choose the lever length that allows strong bracing without breath panic or compensation.
execution_steps: Enter the target position under control instead of jumping or collapsing into it.
Create full-body tension in the target tissues while keeping breathing controlled.
Hold only as long as alignment, pain-free position, and calm bracing remain intact.
Exit the position with the same control used to enter it, then rest before the next set.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the hard part when needed.
Brace without panic breathing.
Exhale through the sticking point or after the rep without collapsing.
common_faults: Chasing fatigue or rep count instead of strength-quality reps.
Holding breath until the athlete panics or shakes out of position.
Changing lever length without keeping the target trunk shape.
quality_gate: No pain or joint collapse.
Start and finish positions match the intended variation.
Final rep looks close to the first rep.
Coach would be comfortable progressing leverage, range, or volume next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Athlete uses momentum, swing, bounce, or impact to survive the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the simplest leverage and shortest range that can be owned. Keep sets short, coach one cue at a time, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add range, pause, or a slightly harder leverage only after the athlete can repeat the setup and finish with control. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress leverage, range, tempo, and total volume gradually. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative range, assistance, higher support, or fewer reps. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding difficulty.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress through harder leverage, longer range, pauses, slow eccentrics, unilateral emphasis, or reduced assistance while preserving adequate rest and strength intent.
scaling.older_adult: Use higher supports, reduced range, slower controlled tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain, breath holding, and positions that irritate joints.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance when appropriate and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, leaking, or pain.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, joint history, and current capacity rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: True Output work if included earlier in the session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only if movement quality remains safe
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the target pattern.
Athlete cannot control bodyweight safely in the chosen variation.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.

ACSM-style resistance training technique and progression guidance
mediaInternalNotes: Film L-Sit from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics or conditioning.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the easier regression without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
Training area and support equipment are stable and clear.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the selected pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe setup for the required support, bar, wall, rings, or floor space.
